items.q: How does fertiliser runoff cause aquatic dead zones?
items.correct: Excess nutrients fuel algal blooms; when algae die, decomposition depletes water oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Fertiliser makes the water too acidic.
items.why: Students assume direct toxicity. Eutrophication is INDIRECT - nutrients cause excess plant growth, then decomposers consume oxygen breaking it down, suffocating fish and other aerobic organisms.

items.q: What is the leading driver of global deforestation?
items.correct: Agriculture (especially cattle ranching, soy, palm oil).
items.why: Students think trees are felled for wood. While logging contributes, the largest driver is land conversion for agriculture - food production drives most tropical forest loss, especially commodities like beef and palm oil.

items.correct: Dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid, lowering ocean pH.
items.misconception: Acid rain falling on the ocean.
items.why: Students think acidification is direct pollution. The main driver is atmospheric CO2 dissolving in seawater - an inevitable result of higher atmospheric concentrations, threatening shell-forming organisms.

items.q: Schaefer's maximum sustainable yield model dates from:
items.why: It's 1954. “1935” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Newfoundland cod fishery moratorium was imposed in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1992. “1972” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate share of consumed fish from aquaculture by 2020 (%)?
items.why: Exact answer: 50 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
